content.text: From Experiment to Improvement

Running a chaos experiment that reveals a gap but produces no follow-through is worse than not running it — it creates a false sense of resilience. The value of chaos engineering is not the experiment itself but the system improvements it motivates.

This sub-subtopic covers the full post-experiment lifecycle: capturing findings, converting them into trackable action items, verifying that fixes hold under the same fault, and feeding results back into the next round of experiments.

attrs.alt: Chaos experiment learning cycle: run → observe → report → action items → verify → re-run

content.text: Classifying Experiment Outcomes
Before writing a post-run report, classify what you observed. Three outcomes are possible:
Outcome
Definition
Follow-up Action
Hypothesis confirmed
System behaved as designed: fallback fired, circuit opened, retry succeeded
Archive experiment; schedule re-run in 90 days to catch regression
Partial failure
System partially recovered but a secondary signal degraded unexpectedly (e.g., fallback worked but latency spiked beyond SLO)
Create action item for the secondary gap; re-run after fix
Hypothesis refuted
System failed to recover: errors escaped canary scope, queue dropped jobs, no alert fired
P1 action item; do not mark experiment as passed until fix is verified

'Hypothesis confirmed' is only valid if every metric you defined in the hypothesis — not just the primary one — was within bounds. A fallback that fires but takes 30 seconds is a partial failure, not a confirmation.
Post-Run Report Structure
Write a post-run report immediately after the experiment ends, while the context is fresh. The report lives in the same repository directory as the experiment spec.

# Chaos Post-Run Report: API Timeout Injection
**Date:** 2026-07-19
**Operator:** alice@example.com
**Experiment ID:** chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01
**Duration:** 02:00–02:28 UTC (28 minutes)

## Hypothesis
When 10% of Claude API calls receive a 35-second timeout, fallback content is served
within 5 seconds and no jobs are dropped from the processing queue.

## Outcome
**Partial failure.**

### What worked
- Fallback activated for 100% of timed-out calls (counter confirmed)
- No jobs dropped from queue (queue depth stayed flat)
- Circuit breaker did NOT open (no consecutive failures — timeouts were non-consecutive at 10% rate)

### What did not work
- Fallback latency: p99 was 8.3 s, exceeding the 5 s hypothesis target
  - Root cause: fallback path made a synchronous DB call not covered by timeout
- Alert `claude_api_timeout_rate > 0.05` did NOT fire during the experiment window
  - Root cause: alert threshold is per-minute rate; 10% of calls spread over 28 min stayed below rate alert threshold

## Metrics During Experiment
| Metric               | Baseline | Peak     | Hypothesis Target |
|----------------------|----------|----------|-------------------|
| Fallback activations | 0/min    | 6.2/min  | > 0               |
| Fallback p99 latency | 0.4 s    | 8.3 s    | < 5 s             |
| Queue depth          | 42       | 47       | < 80% of capacity |
| Non-canary error rate| 0.01%    | 0.02%    | < 0.5%            |
| Alert fired?         | N/A      | NO       | YES               |

## Action Items
| ID     | Finding                          | Owner  | Due Date   | Priority |
|--------|----------------------------------|--------|------------|----------|
| CI-001 | Fallback DB call needs timeout   | @bob   | 2026-07-26 | P1       |
| CI-002 | Alert threshold too coarse       | @alice | 2026-07-30 | P2       |

## Next Run
Re-run after CI-001 and CI-002 are resolved. Target: both hypothesis metrics pass.
Writing Actionable Action Items

An action item from a chaos experiment must be specific enough that someone unfamiliar with the experiment can implement the fix without reading the full report. Vague items like 'improve fallback latency' stall.
Bad Action Item
Why It Stalls
Better Version
Improve fallback latency
No root cause, no scope, no owner
Add 2-second timeout to fallback DB call in workers/claude_handler.py:L87 (fix: use asyncio.wait_for). Owner: @bob. Due: 2026-07-26.
Fix alert
Doesn't specify which alert or what to change
Lower claude_api_timeout_rate alert threshold from 0.10 to 0.02 in monitoring/alerts.yaml:L34. Owner: @alice. Due: 2026-07-30.
Handle truncated responses better
No location, no failure mode defined
Catch ValueError from streaming parser in api/stream_parser.py:L112; log partial_content=true and discard. Owner: @carol. Due: 2026-08-05.
Tracking Action Items to Closure

Open action items from chaos experiments should live in your standard issue tracker (GitHub Issues, Jira, Linear) — not in a separate spreadsheet that no one updates. Tag them with a label so they can be filtered and reviewed as a group.
Create a GitHub label chaos-followup and apply it to all issues generated from experiments.
Add the chaos experiment ID (e.g. chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01) as a label or in the issue title so the issue links back to its source.
Review open chaos-followup issues in the weekly SRE sync; escalate any P1 item that has been open for more than 7 days without a PR.
Mark the experiment as 'not validated' in the experiment registry until all P1 items are resolved and the experiment re-run passes.

# List all open chaos follow-up issues via GitHub CLI
gh issue list --label chaos-followup --state open --json number,title,assignees,dueOn \
  | jq '.[] | {number, title, assignee: .assignees[0].login}'
Verifying Fixes with Re-runs
An action item is closed only after the same experiment passes with all hypothesis metrics satisfied. A code fix that has not been re-tested under the fault condition is untested.
Resolve all P1 action items and merge the fix PRs.
Update the experiment spec if the hypothesis thresholds changed (e.g., you improved fallback p99 to 2 s; update the spec accordingly).
Re-run the experiment from scratch — same blast radius, same canary scope, same stop conditions.
Compare the re-run report to the original. Every finding that produced a P1 action item must now be in the 'what worked' section.
If a new gap appears during the re-run, create new action items and treat this as another iteration.

Do not re-run experiments more than once per day. Each run consumes error budget and stresses the system. Space re-runs by at least the time needed to observe any latent side effects (typically 24 hours).
Feeding Results Into Coverage Planning
Over time, chaos experiments accumulate a body of evidence about which failure modes have been tested and validated. Use this to drive a coverage review every quarter.
Failure Mode
Last Tested
Status
Next Action
API timeout (10%)
Partial — CI-001, CI-002 open
Re-run after fixes
Rate limit injection
Validated
Re-run 2026-09-01 (regression check)
Network partition (60 s)
Validated
Truncated stream
Never
Not tested
Schedule for 2026-08-01
Context overflow
Never
Not tested
Schedule for 2026-08-15
Cascading quota exhaustion
Never
Not tested
Schedule for 2026-09-01
A coverage gap is any failure mode that has never been tested or whose last passing run was more than 90 days ago. Prioritize coverage gaps over re-running already-validated experiments.
Connecting Chaos Results to SLO Reviews
Chaos experiment results belong in your monthly SLO review alongside incident data. They provide two pieces of evidence that incident data alone cannot:
Proof that your system can recover from a fault it has not yet encountered in production — a proactive reliability signal.

Error budget consumption from deliberate experiments — this should be tracked separately from unplanned incidents so you can distinguish 'we consumed budget testing' from 'we consumed budget failing.'
Experiment Registry and Knowledge Base

As your chaos program matures, individual experiment records become a queryable knowledge base. Teams should be able to answer: what have we tested, when did it last pass, and what is our current coverage debt?

An experiment registry is a directory of YAML files in version control, one per experiment type. Each file records the canonical spec, its last run date, its current status, and the IDs of any open action items.

# experiments/api-timeout-injection.yaml
id: api-timeout-injection
title: Claude API Timeout Injection
failure_mode: timeout
description: >
  Inject 35-second delays into 10% of Claude API calls via ChaosClaudeClient wrapper.
  Validates fallback content path and absence of goroutine leaks.

targets:
  - service: claude-worker
    injection_point: sdk-wrapper
    blast_radius_pct: 5    # 5% of workers (1 canary pod)

hypothesis:
  fallback_activates: true
  fallback_p99_seconds: 5.0
  queue_drop_rate: 0.0
  non_canary_error_rate_pct: 0.5

status: partial_failure            # confirmed | partial_failure | refuted | never_run
last_run: "2026-07-19T02:00:00Z"
last_run_id: chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01
open_action_items:
  - CI-001
  - CI-002
next_scheduled_run: null           # set after CI-001 + CI-002 resolved

history:
  - run_id: chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01
    outcome: partial_failure
    report: experiments/reports/chaos-2026-07-19-timeout-01.md
Use a script to generate a coverage dashboard from the registry — scan all YAML files and summarize status. This is the artefact you present at the quarterly chaos review.
Learning Patterns That Repeat Across Experiments

After running five or more experiments, teams typically see recurring patterns in what fails. Recognising these patterns prevents the same class of fix from being rediscovered experiment after experiment.
Recurring Finding
Root Cause Pattern
Systemic Fix
Fallback has high latency
Fallback code path calls a dependency not covered by the same timeout as the primary path
Audit all fallback paths: every external call must have a timeout, and the timeout must be shorter than the primary SLA
Alert does not fire during experiment
Alert threshold uses a rate window that is too wide relative to the experiment duration
Reduce alert evaluation window; test alerts by simulating the fault on a metrics scratchpad before the experiment
Circuit breaker opens too slowly
Failure threshold is set to a consecutive count, but intermittent failures (10% rate) never trigger consecutive failures
Switch to error-rate threshold (e.g., 20% of calls in last 60 s) instead of consecutive-failure count
Partial content written to DB
Parse error caught but transaction already committed partial row
Wrap DB write and Claude API call in the same transaction; rollback on parse error
Retry storm after 429 resolves
All retrying workers fire simultaneously when Retry-After expires
Add jitter to retry delay: sleep(retry_after + random.uniform(0, 5)) instead of sleep(retry_after)

Once a recurring pattern is identified, write a checklist item for the pre-experiment review: 'Does the fallback path have independent timeouts?' A checklist item costs nothing and prevents the same class of finding from appearing in every report.
Summary
Chaos experiment learning closes the loop between fault injection and system improvement. An experiment that reveals a gap is only valuable if that gap is documented, assigned, fixed, and re-tested.
Classify every outcome as confirmed, partial failure, or refuted — partial failures require action items just as full failures do.
Write a post-run report immediately after the experiment using a consistent structure: hypothesis, outcome, metrics table, and action items.
Make action items specific: file, line, exact change, owner, due date. Vague items are never resolved.
Track action items in your standard issue tracker with a chaos-followup label; review them weekly in the SRE sync.
Validate fixes by re-running the same experiment; close the action item only when the re-run passes all hypothesis metrics.
Maintain a coverage table to track which failure modes are validated, stale (> 90 days), or never tested.
Report chaos-consumed error budget separately from incident-consumed budget in SLO reviews.
